findings.description: Off-by-one: `row['stock'] > quantity` rejects a reservation when stock exactly equals the requested quantity; `>=` is required to allow full-stock drawdown.

No guard on `quantity <= 0`: a zero quantity passes the stock check and commits a no-op returning True; a negative quantity subtracts a negative value (i.e., adds stock) and returns True, silently corrupting inventory.

SQL injection: `fragment` is interpolated directly into the query string via an f-string; any caller-supplied value executes arbitrary SQL (defer detail to critic-security, flagged here as a logic/correctness defect too).
`restock()` issues no guard on negative `quantity`: `restock(sku, -10)` silently decrements stock via `stock + (-10)`, corrupting inventory with no error or return value.
`restock()` never checks whether the SKU exists; the UPDATE silently affects zero rows and commits successfully, giving the caller no indication that the SKU was invalid.
N+1 query pattern in `low_stock_report()`: one extra query per low-stock product is issued inside the loop; defer detail to critic-performance, noted here for completeness.
`Product(**row)` unpacks a `sqlite3.Row` by keyword; any future schema column not present in the dataclass (or vice versa) raises a silent `TypeError` at runtime with no defensive handling.
